meta.title: F13 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 6 Series F13 engine — N55, N63, S63, N57, Alpina. Real UK costs, failure data, and honest advice on this grand tourer. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series F13 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F13 engine typically costs £3,500–£18,000 supply only, depending on engine — the N55 six-cylinder sits at £3,500–£6,000, while the Alpina V8 can reach £10,000–£18,000. Fitting adds £780–£1,560. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series F13 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 3.0-litre turbocharged six-cylinder (640i) is the most reliable F13 engine — smooth, responsive, and with a reputation for reliability that makes it the sensible choice. The N57 diesel six (640d) is also a dependable option with strong torque delivery.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series F13?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve stem seal and turbo coolant line failures on the N63 V8 (650i), crank hub and rod bearing issues on the S63 M6, turbocharger and injector issues on the N57 diesel, water pump and VANOS issues on the N55, and — unrelated to the engine — the occasional electronic gremlins and suspension component wear.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 6 Series F13 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability varies significantly by engine choice. The N55 and N57 are the most dependable, while the N63 V8 is complex and expensive to maintain. The S63 in the M6 demands meticulous maintenance. A well-maintained F13 can be a rewarding grand tourer; a neglected one will be expensive.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series F13 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N63 V8 (650i) without meticulous service history — the hot-vee design leads to expensive heat-related failures including valve stem seals and turbo coolant lines. An S63-powered M6 without documented crank hub and rod bearing history is also a risky purchase. No engine is inherently bad, but history is everything.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F13 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Blue smoke on start-up (N63 valve stem seals), whining noise from the engine or drop in power (N57 turbo issues), overheating with no obvious external leak (cooling system failure), or rough idle and check engine lights are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series F13 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £12,000 F13, a £6,000 N63 recon is 50% — borderline but within our 70% BMW threshold. Against a well-kept £20,000+ example, the same recon is 30% — a repair decision. Against a sub-£10,000 example, a V8 engine failure is a write-off. On an M6, the calculus is different — the car's value justifies the S63's high replacement cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 6 Series F13 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 can exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper maintenance. The N57 diesel is similarly durable. The N63 V8 can also reach high mileage but requires meticulous cooling system and valve train maintenance. The S63 is robust but requires crank hub and rod bearing preventative work.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F13 and the E63?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F13 is the third-generation 6 Series Coupé (2011–2018), built on the L6 platform with a range of turbocharged engines — marking the first time the 6 Series used forced induction. The E63 is the second-generation 6 Series (2003–2010), built on the E60 platform with naturally aspirated engines including the N62 V8 and S85 V10 in the M6. The F13 is significantly more refined and technologically advanced.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series F13 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the engine itself. Tell us your variant (640i, 650i, M6, 640d, etc.) and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 6 Series F13 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F13 coupé — from the N57 diesel six to the N63 twin-turbo V8 and the S63-powered M6. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the hot-vee V8 risks that define this generation.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 6 Series F13 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F13 coupé — from the 3.0-litre six-cylinder petrol and diesel units to the 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 in the 650i and M6.

overview.intro: The F13 is the two-door coupé variant of the third-generation BMW 6 Series, unveiled at the 2011 Geneva Motor Show alongside the F12 convertible. Built on the BMW L6 platform shared with the F10 5 Series and F01 7 Series, the F13 introduced a range of turbocharged engines — including the N63 twin-turbo V8 and the S63 in the M6 — marking the first time the M6 used forced induction. Production ran until 2018.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L6 (shared with F10 5 Series, F01 7 Series) • Predecessor: BMW 6 Series E63/E64 • Successor: BMW 8 Series G15 (for body style and class) • Sibling body styles in this generation: F12 (convertible), F06 (Gran Coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: As a low-volume grand tourer that ended production in 2018, the F13 is a niche enthusiast vehicle — the 640i remains the most common variant, while the M6 and 650i are the most sought-after for their V8 performance.

items.quote: The F13's engine range spans three distinct characters. The N55 3.0-litre turbocharged six (640i) is the most dependable engine in the range — smooth, responsive, and with a reputation for reliability that makes it the sensible choice. The N57 3.0-litre diesel six (640d) is the torque king — 308 bhp and 630 Nm of effortless pulling power — and is generally more reliable than the V8 options. The N63 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 (650i) is the most complex — the "hot-vee" design places the turbochargers inside the V of the engine, leading to heat-related failures including valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines, and timing chain issues. The S63 in the M6 is a high-performance twin-turbo V8 with 553 bhp, but it demands meticulous maintenance and has known crank hub and rod bearing concerns. The Alpina B6 is a rare, high-performance variant with up to 600+ bhp. If you're buying an F13, the N55 is the sensible choice; the N57 is the torque-rich alternative; the N63 demands deep pockets; the S63 is for enthusiasts who can absorb the running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines

rows.ownershipVerdict: Improved — N63TU is better but still complex
Most Expensive — outstanding performance, outstanding costs

economicsRule.title: The F13 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£10,000 N63 recon against a £12,000 F13 sits at 50–83% of the car's value — above our 70% BMW threshold on lower-value examples. Against a well-kept £20,000+ example, the same recon drops to 30–50% — a repair decision. Against a sub-£10,000 F13, a V8 engine failure is a write-off. On an M6, the calculus is different — the car's value justifies the S63's high replacement cost. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F13 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N55 640i — simpler six-cylinder with lower running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F13 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N63 650i — hot-vee V8 with expensive heat-related failures.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F13 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: N55 640i — six-cylinder grand touring without the V8 bills.

problems.title: N63 Hot-Vee V8 Heat-Related Failures
problems.rootCause: The N63's "hot-vee" design places the turbochargers inside the V of the engine, generating significant heat. This leads to premature failure of valve stem seals (causing blue smoke on start-up), turbo coolant lines, and the battery drain issues associated with the N63. The N63TU (2013–2018) improved some aspects but retained the hot-vee architecture. Timing chain stretch is also a known issue.
problems.recommendation: If you own an N63-powered F13, budget for valve stem seal replacement as a preventative measure — a £500–£1,200 investment is far cheaper than the £6,000–£11,000 engine replacement that follows a failure. Monitor oil consumption and investigate any blue smoke on start-up immediately.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your F13 650i →

problems.title: S63 Crank Hub & Rod Bearings (M6)
problems.affectedModels: F13 M6, M6 Competition (2012–2018, S63 engine).
problems.rootCause: The S63's crank hub can spin on the crankshaft, causing timing to slip and potentially catastrophic engine damage. The rod bearings can also wear prematurely, particularly on cars that have been tracked or driven hard. Both issues are well-documented in the M community.
problems.recommendation: If you own an F13 M6, budget for a crank hub fix and rod bearing replacement as preventative maintenance. A £1,500–£3,500 investment is far cheaper than the £9,000–£15,000 engine replacement that follows a failure. Use the correct oil and warm the engine properly before hard driving.

cta.label: Compare S63 engine prices for your F13 M6 →

problems.title: N57 Diesel Turbocharger & Injector Issues
problems.rootCause: The N57's twin-turbo setup can suffer from turbocharger failure due to excessive wear and tear, leading to a noticeable drop in power and performance. Fuel injectors can also become clogged or fail, leading to rough idling, decreased fuel efficiency, and poor engine performance.
problems.recommendation: If your 640d experiences a drop in power or rough running, have the turbochargers and injectors inspected immediately. A £500–£1,000 preventative service is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000 repair that follows a failure. Use premium diesel and change the fuel filter regularly.

cta.label: Compare N57 engine prices for your F13 640d →

problems.title: N55 Water Pump & VANOS Solenoids
problems.rootCause: The N55's electric water pump has a finite service life and can fail without warning, leading to overheating. The VANOS solenoids can also become clogged or fail, causing rough idle, reduced power, and check engine lights.
problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump proactively at 60,000–80,000 miles on any N55-powered F13. A £400–£800 preventative job is far cheaper than a £3,500+ engine replacement. Clean the VANOS solenoids at the same time.

cta.label: Compare N55 engine prices for your F13 640i →
